doctrines.rule: A conspiracy agreement need not be explicit; it may be inferred from coordinated conduct, mutual awareness, and circumstantial evidence showing concerted action toward a common objective.
doctrines.split: Courts vary on how much circumstantial evidence suffices: Interstate Circuit allows inference from parallel conduct with mutual awareness; Kotteakos requires proof of a shared stake; Alvarez permits inference from minimal conduct (a nod and smile).
levers: whether defendants knew others were asked to participate
whether conduct represented a departure from normal business practices
whether there is a plausible innocent explanation for the coordinated behavior
duration and frequency of communications between alleged coconspirators
traps: Students assume any coordinated conduct proves agreement, ignoring Kotteakos's requirement that participants share a common purpose beyond dealing with the same central figure.
Students confuse parallel conduct (each acting independently) with concerted action (acting with mutual awareness of others' participation).

doctrines.rule: Under the bilateral approach, conspiracy requires at least two genuinely agreeing minds, so an agreement with an undercover agent who never intends to follow through cannot constitute conspiracy; under the unilateral (MPC) approach, a defendant's belief that the agreement is genuine suffices regardless of the other party's secret intentions.
doctrines.split: Traditional/bilateral (e.g., State v. Pacheco, Valigura) vs. MPC/unilateral (e.g., Garcia v. State); majority of reformed codes adopt unilateral.
levers: whether the defendant's only coconspirator is a government agent
whether any genuine coconspirator exists alongside the agent
whether the jurisdiction follows MPC or traditional approach
traps: Students pick 'no conspiracy' in a unilateral jurisdiction because they focus on the agent's lack of genuine intent, confusing bilateral and unilateral standards.
Students forget that even under the unilateral approach, entrapment remains a separate defense.

doctrines.rule: Most jurisdictions require at least one conspirator to commit an overt act in furtherance of the conspiracy, but the act can be entirely legal and trivial -- far less than the substantial step required for attempt.
doctrines.split: Some serious felony conspiracies (e.g., drug conspiracies under 21 U.S.C. 846) require no overt act at all; MPC requires overt act only for crimes below first/second degree felony.
levers: whether the jurisdiction requires an overt act at all
severity of the target offense
whether the alleged overt act has any connection to the criminal objective
traps: Students apply the attempt 'substantial step' standard to conspiracy overt acts, requiring far more than the law demands.
Students assume the overt act must itself be illegal.

doctrines.rule: Conspiracy requires the defendant to intend to agree (knowingly and voluntarily enter the conspiratorial relationship) and to intend that the object crime be committed; most jurisdictions require purpose even if the underlying crime would be satisfied by a lesser mental state.
doctrines.split: Pond (majority of jurisdictions): conspirator must specifically intend every element of the target offense. Dissent/minority: mens rea for conspiracy should match the mens rea for the substantive offense element-by-element.
levers: whether the target offense contains elements with no specific intent requirement (e.g., weapon display in robbery)
whether the defendant knew of an aggravating factor that elevates the offense
whether the target crime is a general intent or specific intent offense
traps: Students assume conspiracy mens rea mirrors the substantive offense's mens rea element-by-element, missing that Pond requires specific intent as to ALL elements.
Students conclude that one cannot conspire to commit a general intent crime, but Koenig holds otherwise -- the conspirator's purpose to agree and commit the conduct satisfies the requirement.

doctrines.rule: A supplier who knows that a customer is using lawful goods or services for criminal purposes is not a conspirator unless the supplier acts with the purpose of promoting the criminal activity; purpose may be inferred from a stake in the venture, inflated prices, or the aggravated nature of the crime.
doctrines.split: Lauria framework is widely followed, but courts vary on whether the aggravated-crime inference applies to felonies generally or only to the most serious crimes; some jurisdictions collapse knowledge and purpose for serious felonies.
levers: whether the supplier received inflated prices or a share of criminal proceeds
whether the supplier had a legitimate customer base beyond the criminal enterprise
severity of the target crime (misdemeanor vs. serious felony)
whether the supplier took affirmative steps to facilitate (coded messages, special packaging)
volume and proportion of criminal vs. legitimate business
traps: Students equate knowledge with purpose, finding conspiracy whenever the supplier knew of the criminal use.
Students forget Lauria's aggravated-crime exception -- knowledge alone can suffice for grave offenses even without a special stake.

doctrines.rule: Under the Pinkerton doctrine, each conspirator is vicariously liable for the substantive crimes committed by coconspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy, provided those crimes were reasonably foreseeable as a natural consequence of the unlawful agreement.
doctrines.split: Federal courts and some states follow Pinkerton; the MPC and a majority of reformed state codes reject it, requiring traditional accomplice liability elements (aid, abet, encourage) for vicarious liability.
levers: whether the substantive offense was within the scope of the conspiracy
whether the offense was reasonably foreseeable
whether the defendant had withdrawn from the conspiracy before the offense
whether the defendant was incarcerated or otherwise unable to participate when the offense occurred
traps: Students assume Pinkerton applies everywhere, missing that MPC jurisdictions require traditional accomplice liability.
Students apply Pinkerton to unforeseeable crimes, ignoring the foreseeability limitation the majority itself articulated.
Students confuse Pinkerton (vicarious liability from conspiracy membership) with accomplice liability (requires aiding/abetting the specific offense).

doctrines.rule: The distinction between a single conspiracy (wheel with a rim) and multiple separate conspiracies (wheel without a rim) determines joinder, hearsay admissibility, and Pinkerton liability; a single conspiracy requires that all participants share a common purpose and are aware of the broader scheme.
doctrines.split: Courts apply the hub-and-spoke / wheel-and-rim metaphor but disagree on how much mutual awareness among spokes is required to find a rim.
levers: whether peripheral participants knew of each other's involvement
whether participants shared a common stake in the overall enterprise
whether the central figure connected separate transactions into a unified scheme
number of participants charged in a single indictment
traps: Students find a single conspiracy whenever there is a common central figure (the hub), forgetting that Kotteakos requires a rim connecting the spokes.
Students overlook the prejudice from trying separate conspiracies as one: evidence of one group's crimes is admitted against all defendants.

doctrines.rule: A mere agreement to purchase drugs for personal use or resale does not establish a conspiracy to distribute; conspiracy requires evidence of a shared purpose to further distribution, shown by factors such as prolonged dealing, coded language, and credit arrangements like fronting.
levers: duration of the buyer-seller relationship
use of coded language suggesting ongoing coordination
whether the seller extended credit (fronting)
whether the buyer was purchasing for personal use or redistribution
traps: Students classify every repeat drug purchase as conspiracy, missing that routine buyer-seller transactions lack the shared distributive purpose.
Students confuse fronting (credit arrangement indicating mutual investment) with ordinary bulk purchasing.

doctrines.rule: An agreement between two persons to commit a crime that by definition requires the participation of two persons (e.g., dueling, bigamy, bribery) cannot be prosecuted as a conspiracy, unless more persons participate than are logically necessary for the substantive offense.
doctrines.split: The Supreme Court in Iannelli characterized Wharton's Rule as a judicial presumption that legislatures can override, not a constitutional bar.
levers: whether the substantive offense inherently requires two participants
whether more persons joined the scheme than the minimum needed
whether the legislature has expressly authorized conspiracy charges for the offense
traps: Students treat Wharton's Rule as an absolute bar, missing that it is only a presumption that can be overridden by legislative intent.
Students apply Wharton's Rule to crimes that merely happen to involve two people but do not require bilateral participation by definition.

doctrines.rule: When a statute deliberately exempts one class of participants from criminal liability for a bilateral offense, that class cannot be prosecuted for conspiring to commit the offense; the conspiracy statute cannot be used to circumvent the legislature's protective intent.
levers: whether the statute's text or structure demonstrates legislative intent to exempt one party
whether the exempt party's participation is an inseparable incident of the crime
whether the exempt party took affirmative steps beyond passive participation
traps: Students forget Gebardi and charge the protected party with conspiracy, treating the conspiracy statute as a workaround for the substantive statute's exemption.
Students over-extend Gebardi to exempt participants the legislature did not intend to protect.

doctrines.rule: Corporations can be convicted of conspiracy through the acts and agreements of their agents, but individual executive liability requires proof that specific individuals knowingly entered the conspiratorial agreement.
doctrines.split: Courts accept corporate entity guilty pleas for conspiracy while declining to prosecute individual executives, creating an accountability gap; the DOJ's approach to corporate versus individual prosecution remains contested.
levers: whether individual executives had knowledge of the conspiratorial objective
whether the corporation entered a deferred prosecution agreement shielding individuals
whether evidence of agreement among executives was available but not pursued
traps: Students assume that if a corporation is guilty of conspiracy, its executives must be individually liable, ignoring the separate proof requirements for individual culpability.
Students overlook the enforcement asymmetry: conspiracy's procedural advantages are routinely deployed against street-level defendants but rarely against corporate actors.

doctrines.rule: A conspirator who wishes to terminate liability for future substantive offenses must take affirmative action to defeat or disavow the conspiracy's purpose and communicate withdrawal to coconspirators; mere cessation of activity is insufficient.
doctrines.split: Traditional approach requires affirmative steps to defeat the conspiracy plus communication; MPC provides that withdrawal terminates future liability but does not retroactively undo conspiracy liability itself.
levers: whether the defendant took affirmative steps to thwart the conspiracy's objective
whether the defendant communicated withdrawal to all coconspirators
whether the defendant merely stopped participating without affirmative disavowal
timing of withdrawal relative to the substantive offenses
traps: Students treat incarceration as automatic withdrawal, but the Pinkerton court held that imprisonment does not, without more, constitute withdrawal.
Students confuse withdrawal from the conspiracy (which requires affirmative action) with mere abandonment of personal participation.

doctrines.rule: Under traditional law, a defendant may be convicted and punished for both conspiracy and the completed substantive offense; the MPC merger rule prohibits cumulative punishment, treating conspiracy and the target offense as a single course of conduct.
doctrines.split: Traditional (federal) approach allows cumulative punishment; MPC and most reformed codes apply merger.
levers: whether the jurisdiction follows the MPC merger rule
whether the conspiracy had objectives beyond the completed offense
whether the defendant is charged under federal or state law
traps: Students assume merger applies in federal court, where cumulative punishment for conspiracy and the substantive offense is standard.
Students forget that even under merger, conspiracy remains separately punishable if the target offense was not completed.

doctrines.rule: In reverse sting operations, the government supplies the drugs and creates the criminal opportunity; combined with the unilateral conspiracy rule, this allows conviction for conspiring with undercover agents even though no genuine criminal group existed.
doctrines.split: Critics argue manufactured conspiracies undermine the group-danger rationale for conspiracy liability; defenders argue the defendant's demonstrated willingness to participate establishes culpability regardless of the government's role.
levers: whether the government initiated the criminal plan or merely joined an existing one
whether the defendant had predisposition to commit the crime
whether the jurisdiction applies bilateral or unilateral conspiracy
quantity of drugs attributed to the defendant under conspiracy (triggering mandatory minimums)
traps: Students analyze manufactured conspiracies purely as entrapment problems, missing the distinct conspiracy-doctrine issue of whether a genuine agreement existed.
Students forget that under conspiracy's quantity-attribution rule, a peripheral participant faces sentencing based on the total quantity handled by all coconspirators.

doctrines.rule: Traditional conspiracy law and the MPC differ on five key dimensions: agreement standard, mens rea, Pinkerton liability, cumulative punishment, and withdrawal -- the MPC consistently requires higher individual culpability and rejects vicarious liability.
doctrines.split: Federal courts largely follow the traditional approach; most reformed state codes adopt MPC provisions selectively.
levers: which jurisdiction's law applies
whether the question involves Pinkerton liability (rejected by MPC)
whether the question involves merger (adopted by MPC)
whether the question involves unilateral conspiracy (adopted by MPC but contested)
traps: Students apply MPC rules in federal court or vice versa without checking which framework governs.
Students treat the MPC as a monolithic reform, missing that jurisdictions often adopt some MPC provisions while retaining traditional rules on others.

cases.name: State v. Pond
cases.holding: To be convicted of conspiracy, a defendant must specifically intend every element of the target offense, including elements that carry no specific intent requirement for the principal.
cases.facts_summary: Defendant served as lookout during a convenience store robbery where an accomplice gestured as if having a weapon; defendant was convicted of conspiracy to commit robbery in the second degree despite no evidence he intended a weapon would be used.

cases.name: People v. Lauria
cases.holding: A supplier who knows his goods or services are being used for criminal purposes is not a conspirator unless he acts with the purpose of promoting the criminal activity, which may be inferred from a stake in the venture or the aggravated nature of the crime.
cases.facts_summary: Telephone answering service operator knowingly served prostitutes but charged normal rates, had no financial stake in their earnings, and served legitimate customers as well.

cases.name: Pinkerton v. United States
cases.holding: A conspirator is vicariously liable for substantive offenses committed by coconspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy, even without direct participation, provided the offenses were reasonably foreseeable.
cases.facts_summary: Daniel Pinkerton was convicted of substantive tax offenses committed solely by his brother Walter while Daniel was in the penitentiary, based on their ongoing conspiracy to violate revenue laws.

cases.name: Interstate Circuit v. United States
cases.holding: When each party to an alleged conspiracy knows that others are being asked to participate in a coordinated scheme, and all adopt the scheme through a radical departure from prior practice, agreement may be inferred without direct evidence of communication among the parties.
cases.facts_summary: Eight film distributors each received a letter naming all others as addressees, demanding uniform pricing and exhibition restrictions; all complied despite the radical departure from industry practice.

cases.name: Kotteakos v. United States
cases.holding: Where a central figure participates in multiple separate criminal schemes with different groups who have no knowledge of or connection to each other, the evidence establishes multiple conspiracies, not a single conspiracy; trying them as one is prejudicial error.
cases.facts_summary: Loan broker Simon Brown helped numerous unrelated borrowers obtain fraudulent FHA loans; borrowers had no knowledge of each other and shared no common purpose beyond their individual transactions with Brown.

cases.name: United States v. Alvarez
cases.holding: A defendant's promise to be present at a drug unloading site, combined with surrounding circumstances of a marijuana importation scheme, was sufficient for a jury to infer agreement to participate in the conspiracy.
cases.facts_summary: Day laborer Alvarez delivered appliances to an airport where a marijuana importation flight was being arranged, nodded and smiled when asked if he would be at the unloading site, and asked if the agent was going on the plane.

cases.name: Garcia v. State
cases.holding: Under Indiana's unilateral conspiracy statute, a defendant who believes she is entering a genuine agreement to commit a crime is guilty of conspiracy even if her only coconspirator is an undercover agent.
cases.facts_summary: Woman asked a police informant to kill her husband, then paid an undercover detective $200 along with a photograph and record of her husband's daily habits.

cases.name: Gebardi v. United States
cases.holding: When a statute deliberately exempts one class of participants from liability, that class cannot be prosecuted for conspiring to commit the offense.
cases.facts_summary: A man and woman who voluntarily traveled together across state lines were charged with conspiracy to violate the Mann Act, which prohibited transporting a woman for immoral purposes but did not criminalize the woman's voluntary participation.

blocks.summary: Introduces conspiracy as among the most powerful tools in criminal law, distinguishing it from attempt and accomplice liability and noting its prevalence in federal prosecutions.
Uses the Dan Markel murder-for-hire case to illustrate agreement inference from circumstantial evidence, scope disputes, and Pinkerton vicarious liability.
Presents MPC Section 5.03 and 18 U.S.C. Section 371 as the two primary conspiracy frameworks.
Breaks down the elements of conspiracy: agreement, dual intent (intent to agree plus intent that the crime be committed), and the minimal overt act requirement.
Contrasts bilateral and unilateral approaches to the undercover agent problem, with cases illustrating each position and the MPC's rationale for the unilateral approach.
Frames the contested question of what mental state conspiracy requires with respect to each element of the target offense.

State v. Pond holds that conspiracy requires specific intent as to every element of the target offense, including elements carrying no specific intent for the principal; dissent argues this creates an irrational gap requiring higher mens rea for the inchoate crime.
Note on Koenig clarifying that conspiracy can target general intent crimes but not negligence crimes.
Discussion questions testing Pond's holding and applying it to the Markel case (Garcia's intent to kill vs. intent to scare).
Introduces the knowledge-versus-purpose distinction for suppliers who know their goods are used for crime.
People v. Lauria establishes the framework: knowledge alone is insufficient; purpose can be inferred from a stake in the venture or the aggravated nature of the crime.
Discussion questions applying Lauria to Purdue Pharma wholesalers, social media platforms (CSAM, trafficking, fentanyl), and contrasting with Silk Road.
Introduces the Pinkerton doctrine as transforming conspiracy from inchoate offense into vicarious liability theory.
Pinkerton v. United States holds conspirators vicariously liable for coconspirators' foreseeable substantive offenses; Rutledge dissent warns of limitless vicarious criminal liability.
Bank robbery hypothetical demonstrating how Pinkerton expands liability compared to traditional accomplice doctrine, producing near-total conflation of liability.
MPC rejection of Pinkerton: conspirator liability for others' crimes requires traditional accomplice elements, not mere conspiracy membership.
Discussion questions on the partnership analogy, the foreseeability limitation, and whether it meaningfully constrains Pinkerton in drug cases.
Introduction to the agreement-inference progression from Interstate Circuit through Kotteakos to Alvarez.
Interstate Circuit holds that agreement may be inferred when parties know others are invited to join a coordinated scheme and all adopt it through radical departure from prior practice.
Kotteakos distinguishes a wheel with a rim (single conspiracy) from a wheel without a rim (multiple conspiracies) and reverses for prejudicial variance.
Alvarez note case: en banc court finds conspiracy from a nod and smile at a drug importation site; nine-judge dissent warns the net may ensnare the innocent.
Buyer-seller exception: mere purchase agreement does not establish conspiracy; shared distributive purpose required, shown by prolonged dealing, coded language, and fronting.
Structural limits on conspiracy: Wharton's Rule bars conspiracy for inherently bilateral crimes; Gebardi bars conspiracy charges against parties the legislature deliberately exempted.
Purdue Pharma DOJ press release and analysis: corporate entity pleads guilty to conspiracy but no individual executive is convicted of a felony.
Discussion questions comparing the Purdue executives' position on the agreement-inference spectrum and asking why conspiracy's procedural advantages were not deployed against them.
Drug stings and manufactured conspiracy: government use of informants, reverse stings, the girlfriend problem, mandatory minimum interaction, and racial disparities.

Conspiracy as system architecture: how the unilateral rule, substantial step test, mandatory minimums, and civil forfeiture interact to produce manufactured prosecutions with financial incentives and no demonstrated crime-reduction benefit.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests whether a conspirator must specifically intend every element of the target offense, including aggravating factors like weapon use
hypo_seeds.note: Pond's rule creates a gap between substantive and inchoate liability that students must navigate; applying it to the Markel facts (intent to scare vs. intent to kill) tests the boundary.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests the knowledge-versus-purpose line for suppliers who know their goods facilitate crime
hypo_seeds.note: Lauria's three inference factors (stake, absence of legitimate use, aggravated crime) are highly testable; students must identify which factors are present and whether they suffice.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests whether social media platforms with documented knowledge of criminal use have a stake in the venture sufficient for conspiracy liability under Lauria
hypo_seeds.note: Forces students to apply Lauria's framework to modern technology platforms, testing the limits of the stake-in-the-venture and aggravated-crime inferences.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests Pinkerton vicarious liability by contrasting accomplice and conspiracy theories across a multi-member bank robbery scheme
hypo_seeds.note: The A-B-C-D hypothetical is a classic exam vehicle: students must separately analyze liability under accomplice doctrine and Pinkerton, showing how results diverge.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests the foreseeability limitation on Pinkerton and the partnership analogy from Rutledge's dissent
hypo_seeds.note: Asking what crimes are NOT foreseeable in a drug conspiracy exposes how thin the limiting principle is in practice.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests single-versus-multiple conspiracies and the procedural consequences of the distinction
hypo_seeds.note: Students must connect the doctrinal distinction (rim vs. no rim) to concrete procedural consequences: joinder, hearsay admissibility, and Pinkerton scope.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests why agreement inference is applied aggressively against street-level defendants but not corporate executives despite similar circumstantial evidence
hypo_seeds.note: Exposes the enforcement asymmetry: comparing Purdue executives to Alvarez or the Markel defendants on the agreement-inference spectrum.

hypo_seeds.description: Tests the interaction between the girlfriend problem (conspiracy quantity attribution), mandatory minimums, and cooperation incentives
hypo_seeds.note: The girlfriend problem is a rich exam vehicle: peripheral participant sentenced on total quantity, with no information to trade for cooperation.
